perturbation.original_text: a.What is included. "Confidential Information" is non-public information, know-how and trade secrets in any form that: A. Are designated in writing as “confidential” at the time of their disclosure and include : (1) corporate financial records or corporate proprietary information (e.g. trade secrets) exempted under the California Public Records Act, California Government Code Sections 6250, et seq .; or (2) information that a reasonable person knows or reasonably should understand to be confidential , and is treated confidential by the disclosing party. B. The County’s sensitive security information, or technical data, programs, software (including configuration or source codes ), technical information, screen shots, customer information, employee records, computer network, architectural or engineering information, exploitable data, information protected by privacy law, or other information that is treated as confidential by the County, or is prohibited from being disclosed for any reason pursuant to law, statute, regulation, ordinance, or contract .
perturbation.changed_text: a.What is included. "Confidential Information" is non-public information, know-how and trade secrets in any form. Information that a reasonable person knows or reasonably should understand to be confidential , and is treated confidential by the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific categories of information that qualify as "Confidential Information" introduces an in-text contradiction. The modified definition is vague and conflicts with subsequent clauses that assume specific types of data (like employee records or software code) are protected. This creates ambiguity regarding what data the agreement actually protects, especially concerning the scope of what the County considers confidential. Removing the types of information that qualify under the California Public Records Act creates uncertainty if the information is indeed subjected to this agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: b.Security precautions . Each Party agrees: To take reasonable steps to protect the other Party’s Confidential Information. These steps must be at least as protective as those we take to protect our own Confidential Information of a similar nature ; To notify the other promptly upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information; and Attachment A Page 3 of 5 To cooperate with the other to help regain control of the Confidential Information and prevent further unauthorized use or disclosure of it.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' with specific categories and examples. The changed text removes these specific categories, leading to a vague definition. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which outlines security precautions for 'Confidential Information.' Without the specific categories, it is unclear what information the parties are obligated to protect, rendering the security precautions clause ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. The modified definition directly conflicts with the clear expectation in the contradicted text that specific types of data are protected as confidential.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq . If a Party is required to disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information pursuant to applicable law, statute, or regulation , or court order, that Party will give to the other written notice of the order or request mandating disclosure of the Confidential Information in order to provide a reasonable opportunity for the other Party to object to such disclosure of its Confidentia l Information and seek a protective order or appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law. If a Party is required to disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information pursuant to applicable law, statute, or regulation , or court order, that Party will give to the other written notice of the order or request mandating disclosure of the Confidential Information in order to provide a reasonable opportunity for the other Party to object to such disclosure of its Confidentia l Information and seek a protective order or appropriate remedy.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit reference to the California Public Records Act (CPRA) creates an internal contradiction. While the agreement still allows disclosure under legal compulsion, removing the CPRA reference contradicts the section that follows, which details how the County will handle CPRA requests. This creates uncertainty about whether the CPRA is considered under the agreement and, particularly, the county's responsibility when a CPRA request is made
perturbation.contradicted_text: If, in the absence of a protective order, the recipient determines that it is required to disclose such information, it may disclose without breach of this Agreement only Confidential Inform ation specifically required and only to the extent compelled to do so . By entering into this Agreement, Microsoft expressly acknowledges that the County is a government entity subject to the California Public Records Act. If the County receives a request pursuant to the California Public Records Act for Confidential Information that Microsoft has designated as “confidential ” in writing , the County will notify Microsoft of the request, so that Microsoft can seek protection from disclosure by a court of comp etent jurisdiction. Notwithstanding any other provisions of this Agreement, the County may in its discretion disclose Microsoft’s Confidential Information in the County’s possession without breach of this Agreement if the County notifies Microsoft of the pending California Public Records Act request and Microsoft fails to seek and obtain protection from disclosure by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifically mentioned the California Public Records Act (CPRA), clarifying its applicability. The changed text removes this explicit reference. The contradicted text details how the County will handle CPRA requests. By removing the specific reference to the CPRA in the changed text, a contradiction is created. The changed text implies CPRA might not be considered under the agreement, while the contradicted text assumes that the county is subject to the CPRA, and outlines the process when the County receives a CPRA request. This creates a contradiction because it is now unclear whether the CPRA is applicable, directly conflicting with the contradicted section that outlines the procedure when CPRA is in effect.

perturbation.original_text: b. Compliance with law. Each of us will comply with all export laws that apply to Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the sentence: 'Each of us will comply with all export laws that apply to Confidential Information' creates an in-text contradiction if the nature of the confidential information exchanged involves data or technology subject to export regulations (e.g., software). Omitting this compliance clause creates uncertainty and contradiction since failing to comply with applicable export laws would be a breach of contract, but now it is not explicitly enforced. The general compliance is not enough to cover the export regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: a.In general. Subject to the other terms of this Agreement, each of us agrees: Except as required under any court order or law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act and Ralph M. Brown Act (California Government Code section 54950 et seq .), subpoena, or any other legally permitted or required disclosure, the Parties agree not to disclose the other’s Confidential Information to third parties except to those employees of a recipient who are required to have the information for the purposes in this Agreement, Representatives (defined below), and except as otherwise allowed in this Agreement ; and The Parties will use and disclose the other’s Confidential Information only for purposes of evaluating and engaging in an actual or potential business relationship with each other.
perturbation.justification: The original text included a clause (b) requiring compliance with export laws regarding Confidential Information. The changed text omits this clause. The contradicted text (a) generally states that parties agree not to disclose confidential information, with exceptions for legal requirements. However, the omission of the specific export law compliance creates a contradiction because while the general confidentiality clause remains, the explicit requirement to follow export laws, which could dictate how and to whom the information is disclosed, is removed. This is an Omission - In Text Contradiction because removing the specific clause creates uncertainty around export law compliance when the contradicted text assumes protection of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: The County and Microsoft may be referred to individually as “Party” or collectively as “Parties.” “We,” “us” and “our” refer to both of the Parties signing below and the Parties’ respective affilia tes.
perturbation.changed_text: The County and Microsoft may be referred to individually as “Party” or collectively as “Parties.”
perturbation.explanation: Removing the sentence '“We,” “us” and “our” refer to both of the Parties signing below and the Parties’ respective affilia tes.' creates uncertainty in the document about which party is reffering to an action, for example in the section 'The purpose of this Agreement. This Agreement allows us to disclose Confidential Information (defined below) to each other, to our own affiliates and to the other’s affiliates, under the following terms.' In this section it is not clear who is disclosing.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.The purpose of this Agreement. This Agreement allows us to disclose Confidential Information (defined below) to each other, to our own affiliates and to the other’s affiliates, under the following terms. An “affiliate” is any legal entity that one of us owns, that owns one of us or that is under common control with one of us. “Control” and “own” mean possessing a 50% or greater interest in an entity or the right to direct the management of the entity. Additionally we may disclose the other’s Confidential Information to our respective Representatives ( as defined herein below) only if those Representatives are required to have the Confidential Information for the purposes in this Agreement. Before doing so, each Party must ensure that affiliates and Representatives are required to protect the Confidential Information on terms consistent with this Agreement .
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'We,' 'us' and 'our' as referring to both parties and their affiliates. The changed text omits this definition. This omission creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which uses 'us', 'our', and 'we' without a clear referent, thus creating ambiguity as to which party is responsible for actions like disclosing information. The 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' definition applies.

perturbation.original_text: f. Enforceability. If any provision of this Agreement is unenforceable, the parties (or, if we cannot agree, a court) will revise it so that it can be enforced. Even if no revision is possible, the rest of this Agreement will remain in place.
perturbation.changed_text: f. Enforceability. If any provision of this Agreement is unenforceable, the parties (or, if we cannot agree, a court) will revise it so that it can be enforced.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the sentence 'Even if no revision is possible, the rest of this Agreement will remain in place.' creates an in-text contradiction because it now creates uncertainty if the entire agreement will remain enforcebale, if part of it isn't possible. This affects every single section of the agreement, for example the sharing information between affiliates, or the security precautions section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: c.Sharing Confidential Information with affiliates and Representatives. For the purposes of this Agreement, a “Representative” is an employee, contractor, vendor, service provider, advisor or consultant of a Party or a Party’s respective affiliates including, but not limited to, Science Applications International Corporation ( “SAIC”) and Atos Governmental IT Outsourcing Services, LLC (“Atos”) and each of their respective employees , affiliates , and subcontractors . Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information to its Representatives (who may then disclose that Co nfidential Information to other of its Representatives) only if those Representatives are required to have the Confidential Information for the purpose s in this Agreement. Before doing so, each Party must ensure that affiliates and Representatives are required to protect the Confidential Information on terms consistent with this Agreement. Neither Party is required to restrict work assignments of Representatives who have had access to Confidential Information. Each Party acknowledges that they cannot control the incoming information the other Party will disclose in the course of the business relationship between the Parties , or what each Party’s Representatives will remember, even without notes or other aids. Each Party agrees that use of information in its Representatives’ unaided memories in the development or deployment of the Parties’ respective products or services does not create liability for that Party under this Agreement or trade secret law. Both Parties mutually agree to limit what each Party discloses to the other accordingly.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that even if a provision is unenforceable, the rest of the agreement remains in place. The changed text removes this assurance. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text (c. Sharing Confidential Information with affiliates and Representatives) because it introduces uncertainty about whether that section, or any other section, remains enforceable if another part of the agreement is deemed unenforceable. A reasonable person would view the enforceability of the entire agreement to be in question. Therefore, there is a strong in-text contradiction.
